sentences.zh: 自古受命而王，王者之興何嘗不以卜筮決於天命哉！

translations.literal: Since antiquity those who received the mandate became kings; when has the rise of kings ever not been determined by divination according to the mandate of Heaven!
translations.idiomatic: Since ancient times, those who received the mandate have ruled as kings—and when has the rise of any king not been decided through divination and the will of Heaven?

sentences.zh: 其於周尤甚，及秦可見。

translations.literal: This was especially so with Zhou, and also visible with Qin.
translations.idiomatic: This was especially true of the Zhou dynasty, and can be seen clearly in the case of the Qin as well.

sentences.zh: 代王之入，任於卜者。

translations.literal: The King of Dai's accession was entrusted to the diviners.
translations.idiomatic: When the King of Dai ascended to the throne, the matter was entrusted to the diviners.

sentences.zh: 太卜之起，由漢興而有。

translations.literal: The establishment of the Grand Diviner arose with the founding of the Han.
translations.idiomatic: The office of the Grand Diviner was established with the rise of the Han dynasty.

sentences.zh: 司馬季主者，楚人也。

translations.literal: Sima Jizhu was a man of Chu.
translations.idiomatic: Sima Jizhu was a native of Chu.

sentences.zh: 卜於長安東市。

translations.literal: He divined at the eastern market of Chang'an.
translations.idiomatic: He practiced divination in the eastern market of Chang'an.

sentences.zh: 宋忠為中大夫，賈誼為博士，同日俱出洗沐，相從論議，誦易先王聖人之道術，究遍人情，相視而嘆。

translations.literal: Song Zhong served as Palace Grandee and Jia Yi as Erudite; on the same day they both went out on their bathing holiday, accompanied each other in discussion, recited the Changes and the arts of the Way of the former kings and sages, thoroughly investigated human affairs, looked at each other and sighed.
translations.idiomatic: Song Zhong was a Palace Grandee and Jia Yi an Erudite. One day, during their rest day, the two went out together, conversing as they walked. They discussed the Book of Changes and the arts of the Way as practiced by the ancient kings and sages, examining the full breadth of human affairs, until at last they looked at one another and sighed.

sentences.zh: 賈誼曰：「吾聞古之聖人，不居朝廷，必在卜醫之中。

translations.literal: Jia Yi said: 'I have heard that the sages of antiquity, if they did not reside at court, were certainly to be found among the diviners and physicians.'
translations.idiomatic: Jia Yi said, "I have heard it said that the sages of old, if they did not dwell at court, were surely to be found among the diviners and physicians.

sentences.zh: 今吾已見三公九卿朝士大夫，皆可知矣。

translations.literal: Now I have already observed the Three Excellencies, the Nine Ministers, and the court grandees—all of them can be known.
translations.idiomatic: By now I have observed the Three Excellencies, the Nine Ministers, and the officials at court—I know the measure of them all.

sentences.zh: 試之卜數中以觀采。」

translations.literal: Let us test among the diviners to observe their talent.
translations.idiomatic: Let us go among the diviners and see if we can find true talent there."

sentences.zh: 二人即同輿而之市，游於卜肆中。

translations.literal: The two men immediately shared a carriage and went to the market, wandering among the divination stalls.
translations.idiomatic: The two of them shared a carriage and set off for the market, wandering among the divination stalls.

sentences.zh: 天新雨，道少人，司馬季主閒坐，弟子三四人侍，方辯天地之道，日月之運，陰陽吉凶之本。

translations.literal: It had just rained, the roads had few people; Sima Jizhu sat at leisure, attended by three or four disciples, just then discoursing on the Way of Heaven and Earth, the movements of the sun and moon, and the roots of fortune and misfortune in yin and yang.
translations.idiomatic: It had just rained, and the streets were nearly empty. Sima Jizhu sat at his ease, attended by three or four disciples, discoursing on the Way of Heaven and Earth, the movements of the sun and moon, and the origins of fortune and misfortune in the balance of yin and yang.

sentences.zh: 二大夫再拜謁。

translations.literal: The two grandees bowed twice and paid their respects.
translations.idiomatic: The two grandees bowed twice in greeting.

sentences.zh: 司馬季主視其狀貌，如類有知者，即禮之，使弟子延之坐。

translations.literal: Sima Jizhu observed their appearance and bearing; they seemed like men of learning, so he treated them with courtesy and had his disciples invite them to sit.
translations.idiomatic: Sima Jizhu studied their appearance and manner. Seeing that they seemed to be men of learning, he received them courteously and had his disciples invite them to sit.

sentences.zh: 坐定，司馬季主復理前語，分別天地之終始，日月星辰之紀，差次仁義之際，列吉凶之符，語數千言，莫不順理。

translations.literal: When they were seated, Sima Jizhu resumed his earlier discourse, distinguishing the beginning and end of Heaven and Earth, the cycles of the sun, moon, and stars, ordering the gradations of benevolence and righteousness, setting forth the signs of fortune and misfortune—speaking several thousand words, all in accord with principle.
translations.idiomatic: Once they were settled, Sima Jizhu returned to his earlier discourse. He expounded the beginning and the end of Heaven and Earth, the cycles of the sun, moon, and stars, the gradations of benevolence and righteousness, and the tokens of fortune and misfortune. He spoke for several thousand words, and every one accorded with reason.

sentences.zh: 宋忠、賈誼瞿然而悟，獵纓正襟危坐，曰：「吾望先生之狀，聽先生之辭，小子竊觀於世，未嘗見也。

translations.literal: Song Zhong and Jia Yi were startled into understanding, straightened their cap strings, adjusted their lapels, and sat upright, saying: 'Gazing upon your appearance, sir, and listening to your words—in our humble observation of the world, we have never seen the like.'
translations.idiomatic: Song Zhong and Jia Yi were startled into awareness. They straightened their cap strings, adjusted their lapels, and sat upright. "Gazing upon your bearing, sir, and listening to your words," they said, "in all our years observing the world, we have never encountered anyone like you.

sentences.zh: 今何居之卑，何行之汙？」

translations.literal: Why then is your position so lowly, and your occupation so degraded?
translations.idiomatic: Why, then, do you dwell in so lowly a station and follow so degraded a profession?"

sentences.zh: 司馬季主捧腹大笑曰：「觀大夫類有道術者，今何言之陋也，何辭之野也！

translations.literal: Sima Jizhu held his belly and laughed heartily, saying: 'Looking at you grandees, you seem like men who possess the Way and its arts, yet how crude are your words, how coarse your language!'
translations.idiomatic: Sima Jizhu threw back his head, holding his belly in a great laugh. "Looking at you, my lords, you appear to be men who understand the Way and its arts—yet how crude your words are, and how coarse your speech!

sentences.zh: 今夫子所賢者何也？

translations.literal: Now what is it that you, sirs, consider worthy?
translations.idiomatic: Tell me, what is it that you consider worthy?

sentences.zh: 所高者誰也？

translations.literal: Whom do you hold in high regard?
translations.idiomatic: Whom do you hold in high esteem?

sentences.zh: 今何以卑汙長者？」

translations.literal: Why then do you call your elders lowly and degraded?
translations.idiomatic: On what grounds do you call your elders lowly and degraded?"

sentences.zh: 二君曰：「尊官厚祿，世之所高也，賢才處之。

translations.literal: The two lords said: 'High office and generous stipends are what the world esteems; men of talent and worth occupy them.'
translations.idiomatic: The two lords replied, "Exalted offices and generous stipends—these are what the world holds in high esteem, and men of talent and worth occupy them.

sentences.zh: 今所處非其地，故謂之卑。

translations.literal: Now your position is not in its proper place, hence we call it lowly.
translations.idiomatic: You do not occupy the station befitting such a man, and so we call your position lowly.

sentences.zh: 言不信，行不驗，取不當，故謂之汙。

translations.literal: Your words are not trusted, your practices are not verified, your fees are not deserved—hence we call it degraded.
translations.idiomatic: Your words are not believed, your methods are not proven, and your fees are not deserved—and so we call your work degraded.

sentences.zh: 夫卜筮者，世俗之所賤簡也。

translations.literal: Diviners are those whom the vulgar world despises and looks down upon.
translations.idiomatic: Diviners are despised and belittled by the common world.

sentences.zh: 世皆言曰：『夫卜者多言誇嚴以得人情，虛高人祿命以說人志，擅言禍災以傷人心，矯言鬼神以盡人財，厚求拜謝以私於己。』

translations.literal: Everyone in the world says: 'Diviners use extravagant and intimidating words to win people's confidence, falsely exalt people's fortunes and destinies to please their ambitions, presumptuously speak of calamities to wound their hearts, falsely invoke ghosts and spirits to drain their wealth, and demand generous gifts of gratitude to enrich themselves.'
translations.idiomatic: Everyone says, 'Diviners use extravagant and intimidating words to gain people's trust. They inflate people's fortunes with empty flattery to gratify their ambitions, conjure calamities from thin air to wound their hearts, invoke spirits and ghosts under false pretenses to drain their wealth, and demand lavish gifts of thanks to line their own pockets.'

sentences.zh: 此吾之所恥，故謂之卑汙也。」

translations.literal: This is what we are ashamed of, hence we call it lowly and degraded.
translations.idiomatic: This is what we find shameful, and that is why we call the profession lowly and degraded."

sentences.zh: 司馬季主曰：「公且安坐。

translations.literal: Sima Jizhu said: 'Please remain seated, sirs.'
translations.idiomatic: Sima Jizhu said, "Please, sirs, remain seated.

sentences.zh: 公見夫被髪童子乎？

translations.literal: Have you seen a child with disheveled hair?
translations.idiomatic: Have you ever observed a child with unkempt hair?

sentences.zh: 日月照之則行，不照則止，問之日月疵瑕吉凶，則不能理。

translations.literal: When the sun and moon shine on him he walks; when they do not shine he stops. Ask him about the blemishes of the sun and moon, or about fortune and misfortune, and he cannot reason it out.
translations.idiomatic: When the sun and moon shine, he walks; when they do not, he stops. But ask him about the eclipses of the sun and moon, or about the nature of fortune and misfortune, and he cannot make sense of it.

sentences.zh: 由是觀之，能知別賢與不肖者寡矣。

translations.literal: From this we can see that those who can distinguish the worthy from the unworthy are few indeed.
translations.idiomatic: From this it is clear that few in this world can truly tell the worthy from the unworthy."

sentences.zh: 「賢之行也，直道以正諫，三諫不聽則退。

translations.literal: The conduct of the worthy is to follow the straight path and offer upright remonstrance; if after three remonstrances one is not heeded, then to withdraw.
translations.idiomatic: "The conduct of a worthy man is this: he follows the straight path and offers upright counsel. If after three remonstrances he is not heeded, he withdraws.

sentences.zh: 其譽人也不望其報，惡人也不顧其怨，以便國家利眾為務。

translations.literal: When he praises a man he does not expect repayment; when he criticizes a man he does not fear his resentment. He devotes himself to benefiting the state and the people.
translations.idiomatic: When he commends someone, he does not look for repayment. When he condemns someone, he does not shrink from the resentment it brings. His sole concern is the benefit of the state and the people.

sentences.zh: 故官非其任不處也，祿非其功不受也；

translations.literal: Therefore he will not occupy an office unless it matches his duties, nor accept a stipend unless it matches his merits.
translations.idiomatic: He will not hold an office that does not match his abilities, nor accept a stipend that does not match his merits.

sentences.zh: 見人不正，雖貴不敬也；

translations.literal: When he sees a man who is not upright, even if the man is noble he does not show respect.
translations.idiomatic: If he encounters a man who is not upright, he will not show him respect, however noble the man may be.

sentences.zh: 見人有污，雖尊不下也；

translations.literal: When he sees a man who is tainted, even if the man is of high rank he does not defer to him.
translations.idiomatic: If he encounters a man who is tainted, he will not defer to him, however exalted his rank.

sentences.zh: 得不為喜，去不為恨；

translations.literal: He does not rejoice at gaining office, nor resent losing it.
translations.idiomatic: He does not rejoice at gaining office, nor harbor resentment at losing it.

sentences.zh: 非其罪也，雖累辱而不愧也。

translations.literal: If it is not his fault, even though repeatedly humiliated he feels no shame.
translations.idiomatic: When the fault is not his own, he feels no shame, no matter how often he is humiliated.

sentences.zh: 「今公所謂賢者，皆可為羞矣。

translations.literal: Now those whom you call worthy—all of them are a cause for shame.
translations.idiomatic: But those whom you call worthy—every one of them should be a cause for shame.

sentences.zh: 卑疵而前，孅趨而言；

translations.literal: They bow and scrape to advance, they mince forward to speak.
translations.idiomatic: They grovel and fawn to get ahead, mincing forward to have their say.

sentences.zh: 相引以勢，相導以利；

translations.literal: They draw one another in by power, and lead one another on with profit.
translations.idiomatic: They pull one another along by the leverage of power and lead one another on with the lure of profit.

sentences.zh: 比周賓正，以求尊譽，以受公奉；

translations.literal: They form cliques and factions, marginalize the upright, in order to seek honor and reputation, and to receive public emoluments.
translations.idiomatic: They form cliques and factions, pushing aside the upright, all to pursue honors and reputation and to draw public stipends.

sentences.zh: 事私利，枉主法，獵農民；

translations.literal: They serve their private interests, pervert the ruler's laws, and prey upon the farming people.
translations.idiomatic: They serve their own private interests, twist the ruler's laws to their purposes, and prey upon the common farmers.

sentences.zh: 以官為威，以法為機，求利逆暴：譬無異於操白刃劫人者也。

translations.literal: They use their office as a weapon of authority and the law as a tool, seeking profit through violence and tyranny—they are no different from those who hold bare blades and rob people.
translations.idiomatic: They wield their office as a bludgeon and the law as a trap, pursuing profit through violence and oppression. They are no different from brigands who hold bare blades to rob people.

sentences.zh: 初試官時，倍力為巧詐，飾虛功執空文以罔主上，用居上為右；

translations.literal: When they first enter office they redouble their efforts in cunning and deceit, embellish false achievements and present empty documents to deceive the sovereign, and thereby secure the highest positions.
translations.idiomatic: When they first enter office, they redouble their efforts at cunning and deceit. They embellish phantom achievements and brandish empty documents to hoodwink the sovereign, and by such means secure the highest positions.

sentences.zh: 試官不讓賢陳功，見偽增實，以無為有，以少為多，以求便勢尊位；

translations.literal: When examined for office they do not yield to the worthy or display real merit; they present the false as genuine, make something from nothing, and turn the little into much, all to seek advantageous power and honored positions.
translations.idiomatic: When evaluated for office, they never yield to the worthy or present real merits. They pass off the false as genuine, conjure something from nothing, and inflate the meager into abundance—all to secure positions of power and prestige.

sentences.zh: 食飲驅馳，從姬歌兒，不顧於親，犯法害民，虛公家：此夫為盜不操矛弧者也，攻而不用弦刃者也，欺父母未有罪而弒君未伐者也。

translations.literal: They feast and drink, race about in chariots, keep company with concubines and singing girls, disregard their own kin, break the law and harm the people, and drain the public coffers. They are thieves who do not carry spears and bows, raiders who do not use bowstrings and blades, men who deceive their parents without being charged and slay their sovereign without being punished.
translations.idiomatic: They feast and carouse, race their carriages, keep company with concubines and singing girls, neglect their own kin, break the law and harm the people, and drain the public coffers. These are thieves who carry no spears or bows, marauders who wield no bowstrings or blades—men who betray their parents without being charged and murder their sovereign without being punished.

sentences.zh: 何以為高賢才乎？

translations.literal: How can these be considered exalted men of talent and worth?
translations.idiomatic: How can such men be called exalted and worthy?"

sentences.zh: 「盜賊發不能禁，夷貊不服不能攝，姦邪起不能塞，官秏亂不能治，四時不和不能調，歲穀不孰不能適。

translations.literal: When bandits and thieves arise they cannot suppress them; when the Yi and Mo barbarians do not submit they cannot overawe them; when treachery and wickedness spring up they cannot stop them; when government is wasteful and disordered they cannot set it right; when the four seasons are out of harmony they cannot regulate them; when the year's grain does not ripen they cannot remedy it.
translations.idiomatic: "When bandits arise, they cannot suppress them. When the barbarian tribes refuse to submit, they cannot overawe them. When treachery and wickedness spring up, they cannot check them. When the government falls into waste and disorder, they cannot set it right. When the four seasons fall out of harmony, they cannot restore the balance. When the year's grain fails to ripen, they cannot find a remedy.

sentences.zh: 才賢不為，是不忠也；

translations.literal: If one has the talent of a worthy and yet does not act, that is disloyalty.
translations.idiomatic: If a man possesses the talent of a worthy yet does nothing, that is disloyalty.

sentences.zh: 才不賢而託官位，利上奉，妨賢者處，是竊位也；

translations.literal: If one lacks the talent of a worthy yet clings to office, profits from the sovereign's stipend, and blocks the worthy from their rightful places, that is theft of position.
translations.idiomatic: If a man lacks the talent of a worthy yet clings to his office, profits from the sovereign's stipend, and blocks the worthy from their rightful places—that is the theft of a position.

sentences.zh: 有人者進，有財者禮，是偽也。

translations.literal: To advance those who have connections and show courtesy to those who have wealth—that is hypocrisy.
translations.idiomatic: To promote those with connections and treat with courtesy only those who have wealth—that is hypocrisy.

sentences.zh: 子獨不見鴟梟之與鳳皇翔乎？

translations.literal: Have you alone not seen owls soaring alongside the phoenix?
translations.idiomatic: Have you not seen the owls soaring side by side with the phoenix?

sentences.zh: 蘭芷芎藭棄於廣野，蒿蕭成林，使君子退而不顯眾，公等是也。

translations.literal: Orchid, angelica, and lovage are cast aside in the open wilds while mugwort and artemisia form forests, causing the noble man to retreat and not be prominent among the multitude—you sirs are just so.
translations.idiomatic: Orchids, angelica, and lovage are cast aside in the open wilds while mugwort and artemisia grow into thickets. The noble man withdraws and is lost among the crowd—and you, sirs, are the very proof of it."

sentences.zh: 「述而不作，君子義也。

translations.literal: To transmit and not to create—this is the righteousness of the noble man.
translations.idiomatic: "To transmit and not to innovate—that is the principle of the noble man.

sentences.zh: 今夫卜者，必法天地，象四時，順於仁義，分策定卦，旋式正棋，然後言天地之利害，事之成敗。

translations.literal: Now the diviner must model himself upon Heaven and Earth, pattern himself after the four seasons, accord with benevolence and righteousness, sort the milfoil stalks, determine the hexagram, turn the cosmograph, and set the pieces in order—only then does he speak of the advantages and harms of Heaven and Earth, and the success or failure of affairs.
translations.idiomatic: The diviner must take Heaven and Earth as his model, pattern himself after the four seasons, and accord with benevolence and righteousness. He sorts the milfoil stalks, determines the hexagram, turns the cosmograph, and arranges the pieces—only then does he pronounce upon the benefits and harms of Heaven and Earth, and the success or failure of human affairs.

sentences.zh: 昔先王之定國家，必先龜策日月，而後乃敢代；

translations.literal: In the past when the former kings established their states, they always first consulted the tortoise shell and milfoil stalks regarding the sun and moon, and only then dared to take power.
translations.idiomatic: In ancient times, when the former kings established their states, they always first consulted the tortoise shell and milfoil stalks to read the heavens, and only then dared to assume power.

sentences.zh: 正時日，乃后入家；

translations.literal: They determined the correct time and day, and only then entered their households.
translations.idiomatic: They determined the auspicious day, and only then entered their new households.

sentences.zh: 產子必先占吉凶，后乃有之。

translations.literal: When a child was to be born they always first divined fortune and misfortune, and only then had the child.
translations.idiomatic: Before a child was born, they always first divined its fortune, and only then welcomed it into the world.

sentences.zh: 自伏羲作八卦，周文王演三百八十四爻而天下治。

translations.literal: Since Fuxi created the Eight Trigrams and King Wen of Zhou elaborated the three hundred eighty-four lines, the realm has been well governed.
translations.idiomatic: Ever since Fuxi created the Eight Trigrams and King Wen of Zhou elaborated the three hundred and eighty-four lines, the realm has been governed in good order.

sentences.zh: 越王句踐放文王八卦以破敵國，霸天下。

translations.literal: King Goujian of Yue followed King Wen's Eight Trigrams to defeat enemy states and dominate the realm.
translations.idiomatic: King Goujian of Yue followed King Wen's use of the Eight Trigrams to vanquish his enemies and become the hegemon of the realm.

sentences.zh: 由是言之，卜筮有何負哉！

translations.literal: From this perspective, how has divination ever failed anyone!
translations.idiomatic: Seen in this light, how has divination ever failed?"

sentences.zh: 「且夫卜筮者，埽除設坐，正其冠帶，然後乃言事，此有禮也。

translations.literal: Moreover the diviner sweeps and cleans, sets out the seats, straightens his cap and sash, and only then speaks of affairs—this is propriety.
translations.idiomatic: "Moreover, the diviner sweeps and prepares the space, sets out the seats, straightens his cap and sash, and only then speaks of the matter at hand—this is propriety.

sentences.zh: 言而鬼神或以饗，忠臣以事其上，孝子以養其親，慈父以畜其子，此有德者也。

translations.literal: Through his words the ghosts and spirits may receive offerings, loyal ministers may serve their superiors, filial sons may support their parents, and loving fathers may raise their children—this is a man of virtue.
translations.idiomatic: Through his words, the spirits receive their offerings, loyal ministers serve their rulers, filial sons support their parents, and loving fathers nurture their children—this is virtue.

sentences.zh: 而以義置數十百錢，病者或以愈，且死或以生，患或以免，事或以成，嫁子娶婦或以養生：此之為德，豈直數十百錢哉！

translations.literal: And for the righteous fee of a few tens or hundreds of coins, the sick may be healed, those at death's door may live, calamities may be avoided, undertakings may succeed, and sons may be married and wives taken to sustain life—is this virtue worth merely a few tens or hundreds of coins!
translations.idiomatic: For the fair fee of a few tens or hundreds of coins, the sick may be healed, the dying may survive, calamities may be averted, ventures may succeed, and marriages may be arranged to carry on the family line. Is such virtue worth merely a few tens or hundreds of coins?

sentences.zh: 此夫老子所謂『上德不德，是以有德』。

translations.literal: This is what Laozi meant by 'The highest virtue does not consider itself virtuous, and so it truly possesses virtue.'
translations.idiomatic: This is what Laozi meant when he said, 'The highest virtue does not regard itself as virtuous—and therefore truly possesses virtue.'

sentences.zh: 今夫卜筮者利大而謝少，老子之云豈異於是乎？

translations.literal: Now the diviner provides great benefit for a small fee—is this not exactly what Laozi was speaking of?
translations.idiomatic: The diviner provides great benefit in exchange for a small fee. Is this not precisely what Laozi had in mind?"

sentences.zh: 「莊子曰：『君子內無饑寒之患，外無劫奪之憂，居上而敬，居下不為害，君子之道也。』

translations.literal: Zhuangzi said: 'The noble man has within no worry of hunger and cold, and without no fear of robbery and seizure; when he is in a high position he is revered, when in a low position he does no harm—this is the Way of the noble man.'
translations.idiomatic: "Zhuangzi said, 'The noble man suffers no hunger or cold within, and fears no robbery or plunder without. In a high position he is respected; in a low position he does no harm. This is the Way of the noble man.'

sentences.zh: 今夫卜筮者之為業也，積之無委聚，藏之不用府庫，徙之不用輜車，負裝之不重，止而用之無盡索之時。

translations.literal: Now as for the diviner's trade: when accumulated it forms no heaps, when stored it requires no treasury, when moved it needs no baggage wagons, when carried it is not heavy, and when one stops to use it there is never a time when it runs out.
translations.idiomatic: Consider the diviner's trade: when hoarded it requires no granary, when stored it needs no treasury, when moved it calls for no baggage wagons, and when shouldered it weighs nothing. Wherever one stops to practice it, it never runs dry.

sentences.zh: 持不盡索之物，游於無窮之世，雖莊氏之行未能增於是也，子何故而云不可卜哉？

translations.literal: Possessing an inexhaustible thing and wandering through a boundless world—even the conduct of Master Zhuang could not surpass this. Why then do you say that divination is no good?
translations.idiomatic: To possess an inexhaustible thing and wander through a boundless world—even the conduct of Master Zhuang himself could add nothing to this. Why, then, do you insist that divination is not a worthy pursuit?

sentences.zh: 天不足西北，星辰西北移；

translations.literal: Heaven is lacking in the northwest, and the stars shift to the northwest.
translations.idiomatic: Heaven is wanting in the northwest, and the stars drift toward the northwest.

sentences.zh: 地不足東南，以海為池；

translations.literal: Earth is lacking in the southeast, and the sea fills it as a pool.
translations.idiomatic: Earth is wanting in the southeast, and the sea fills the gap.

sentences.zh: 日中必移，月滿必虧；

translations.literal: The sun at its zenith must move on; the moon when full must wane.
translations.idiomatic: The sun at its zenith inevitably declines; the full moon inevitably wanes.

sentences.zh: 先王之道，乍存乍亡。

translations.literal: The Way of the former kings, now preserved and now lost.
translations.idiomatic: Even the Way of the former kings now flourishes and now fades.

sentences.zh: 公責卜者言必信，不亦惑乎！

translations.literal: You demand that the diviner's words always prove true—is that not deluded!
translations.idiomatic: You demand that a diviner's words always prove true—are you not deluding yourselves?"

sentences.zh: 「公見夫談士辯人乎？

translations.literal: Have you seen the rhetoricians and debaters?
translations.idiomatic: "Have you observed the rhetoricians and debaters?

sentences.zh: 慮事定計，必是人也，然不能以一言說人主意，故言必稱先王，語必道上古；

translations.literal: When deliberating on affairs and formulating plans, it must be such men; yet they cannot persuade the ruler's mind with a single word, and so their speeches always invoke the former kings and their discourse always refers to high antiquity.
translations.idiomatic: When plans must be deliberated and strategies devised, these are the very men for it. Yet they cannot sway the ruler's mind with a single word, and so their speeches always invoke the former kings and their discourse always reaches back to high antiquity.

sentences.zh: 慮事定計，飾先王之成功，語其敗害，以恐喜人主之志，以求其欲。

translations.literal: In deliberating on affairs and formulating plans, they embellish the former kings' achievements and speak of failures and harms, in order to frighten or please the ruler's ambitions and thereby obtain what they desire.
translations.idiomatic: In deliberating on affairs and devising plans, they embellish the achievements of the former kings and hold forth on the calamities of the past, playing upon the ruler's fears and ambitions to obtain what they desire.

sentences.zh: 多言誇嚴，莫大於此矣。

translations.literal: For extravagant and intimidating speech, nothing surpasses this.
translations.idiomatic: When it comes to extravagant and intimidating speech, nothing surpasses this.

sentences.zh: 然欲彊國成功，盡忠於上，非此不立。

translations.literal: Yet if one wishes to strengthen the state, achieve success, and be fully loyal to the sovereign, it cannot be accomplished without such methods.
translations.idiomatic: Yet if one wishes to strengthen the state, achieve great things, and show utmost loyalty to the sovereign, these methods are indispensable.

sentences.zh: 今夫卜者，導惑教愚也。

translations.literal: Now the diviner guides the confused and instructs the foolish.
translations.idiomatic: The diviner guides the confused and instructs the ignorant.

sentences.zh: 夫愚惑之人，豈能以一言而知之哉！

translations.literal: Can the foolish and confused be made to understand with a single word!
translations.idiomatic: How can the confused and ignorant be made to understand with a single word?

sentences.zh: 言不厭多。

translations.literal: Words are never too many.
translations.idiomatic: One can never use too many words."

sentences.zh: 「故騏驥不能與罷驢為駟，而鳳皇不與燕雀為群，而賢者亦不與不肖者同列。

translations.literal: Therefore the thoroughbred cannot be yoked in a team with a worn-out donkey, the phoenix does not flock with sparrows, and the worthy does not stand in the same ranks as the unworthy.
translations.idiomatic: "A thoroughbred cannot be yoked in a team with a worn-out donkey, the phoenix does not flock with sparrows, and the worthy do not stand in the same ranks as the unworthy.

sentences.zh: 故君子處卑隱以辟眾，自匿以辟倫，微見德順以除群害，以明天性，助上養下，多其功利，不求尊譽。

translations.literal: Therefore the noble man dwells in a lowly and hidden position to avoid the crowd, conceals himself to avoid his peers, subtly displays virtue and compliance to eliminate the harms of the multitude, to illuminate the nature of Heaven, to assist those above and nourish those below, to multiply benefits, and yet does not seek honor or glory.
translations.idiomatic: The noble man therefore dwells in a humble and hidden station to avoid the crowd, conceals himself to escape the company of his peers, and subtly manifests virtue and compliance to rid the world of its ills. He illuminates the nature of Heaven, assists those above and nourishes those below, multiplies the benefits to all—yet seeks neither honor nor glory.

sentences.zh: 公之等喁喁者也，何知長者之道乎！」

translations.literal: You sirs are merely chattering fish—what do you know of the way of the elders!
translations.idiomatic: You and your kind are nothing but chattering fish—what can you know of the Way of the elders?"

sentences.zh: 宋忠、賈誼忽而自失，芒乎無色，悵然噤口不能言。

translations.literal: Song Zhong and Jia Yi were suddenly overcome and at a loss; their faces went blank and colorless, and in dejection they clamped their mouths shut and could not speak.
translations.idiomatic: Song Zhong and Jia Yi were overcome with shame. The color drained from their faces, and they could only sit in dejected silence, unable to utter a word.

sentences.zh: 於是攝衣而起，再拜而辭。

translations.literal: Thereupon they gathered up their robes and rose, bowed twice, and took their leave.
translations.idiomatic: They gathered their robes, rose to their feet, bowed twice, and took their leave.

sentences.zh: 行洋洋也，出門僅能自上車，伏軾低頭，卒不能出氣。

translations.literal: Their gait was unsteady; upon reaching the door they could barely climb into their carriage. They leaned against the crossbar with heads bowed, unable to catch their breath.
translations.idiomatic: They walked in a daze. At the door, they could barely manage to climb into their carriage. They slumped against the crossbar with heads bowed, unable even to draw a breath.

sentences.zh: 居三日，宋忠見賈誼於殿門外，乃相引屏語相謂自嘆曰：「道高益安，勢高益危。

translations.literal: After three days Song Zhong met Jia Yi outside the palace gate; they drew each other aside to speak privately and lamented to one another, saying: 'The higher one's moral cultivation, the greater one's peace; the higher one's political power, the greater one's peril.'
translations.idiomatic: Three days later, Song Zhong encountered Jia Yi outside the palace gate. They drew each other aside and spoke in private, sighing to one another: "The higher one's moral cultivation, the greater one's peace; the higher one's political power, the greater one's peril.

sentences.zh: 居赫赫之勢，失身且有日矣。
夫卜而有不審，不見奪糈；
為人主計而不審，身無所處。
此相去遠矣，猶天冠地屨也。
此老子之所謂『無名者萬物之始』也。
天地曠曠，物之熙熙，或安或危，莫知居之。
我與若，何足預彼哉！
彼久而愈安，雖曾氏之義未有以異也。」
久之，宋忠使匈奴，不至而還，抵罪。
而賈誼為梁懷王傅，王墮馬薨，誼不食，毒恨而死。
此務華絕根者也。
太史公曰：古者卜人所以不載者，多不見于篇。
及至司馬季主，余志而著之。
褚先生曰：臣為郎時，游觀長安中，見卜筮之賢大夫，觀其起居行步，坐起自動，誓正其衣冠而當鄉人也，有君子之風。
見性好解婦來卜，對之顏色嚴振，未嘗見齒而笑也。
從古以來，賢者避世，有居止舞澤者，有居民閒閉口不言，有隱居卜筮閒以全身者。
夫司馬季主者，楚賢大夫，游學長安，通易經，術黃帝、老子，博聞遠見。
觀其對二大夫貴人之談言，稱引古明王聖人道，固非淺聞小數之能。
及卜筮立名聲千里者，各往往而在。
傳曰：「富為上，貴次之；
既貴各各學一伎能立其身。」
黃直，大夫也；
陳君夫，婦人也：以相馬立名天下。
齊張仲、曲成侯以善擊刺學用劍，立名天下。
留長孺以相彘立名。
滎陽褚氏以相牛立名。
能以伎能立名者甚多，皆有高世絕人之風，何可勝言。
故曰：「非其地，樹之不生；
非其意，教之不成。」
夫家之教子孫，當視其所以好，好含茍生活之道，因而成之。
故曰：「制宅命子，足以觀士；
子有處所，可謂賢人。」
臣為郎時，與太卜待詔為郎者同署，言曰：「孝武帝時，聚會占家問之，某日可取婦乎？
五行家曰可，堪輿家曰不可，建除家曰不吉，叢辰家曰大凶，歷家曰小凶，天人家曰小吉，太一家曰大吉。
辯訟不決，以狀聞。
制曰：『避諸死忌，以五行為主。』」
人取於五行者也。
